distribution_driven: Hybrid, leaning function/configuration-driven. The break is not derived automatically from the distribution; the developer manually declares subdomains (e.g. [[0,1000],[1000,10000],[10000,100000]]) and the fraction of range each occupies via scope (e.g. [[0,0.25],[0.25,0.75],[0.75,1]]). Within each subdomain a normal continuous (linear) scale applies. So the choice of where to break is informed by the skew, but the scale itself behaves as a piecewise linear function once configured.
d3_api: External plugin — not core D3. Package: d3-scale-break (npm; also seen referenced as @lukewhyte/d3-scale-break on Observable). API mirrors d3-scale: construct a broken scale, then .domain([...]) accepting multiple subdomains such as [[0,1000],[1000,10000],[10000,100000]], .range([...]), and a .scope([...]) method controlling how much of the pixel range each subscale occupies (e.g. [[0,0.25],[0.25,0.75],[0.75,1]]). Provides axis helpers analogous to D3's: axisLeft, axisRight, axisTop, axisBottom that render a broken axis (compressed gap, typically near zero). [uncertain] Exact constructor name and full chained signature not verified against current source; verify against the README before use.
library_dependency: Plugin dependency (option b). Requires installing d3-scale-break (npm version ~0.1.11, pre-1.0). Adds bundle weight and, critically, maintenance risk: the repo is at v0.x and effectively unmaintained (last push Jan 2023). It is a thin wrapper over d3-scale concepts but is not part of the official D3 distribution.
zero_negative_handling: Effectively safe at zero and negative because each subdomain uses linear (not log) interpolation internally, so there is no <=0 failure mode like scaleLog. The typical use compresses a gap starting at or near zero. [uncertain] Behaviour with explicitly negative subdomains is not documented and not verified.
authority_verdict: Broken-axis / scale-break techniques are treated cautiously by authorities. Cleveland ('The Elements of Graphing Data', 1985/1994) warns that axis breaks impede the visual comparison of magnitudes and can mislead; the canonical guidance is to prefer a full-panel break (two clearly separated panels) over a slashed/compressed single axis when a break is unavoidable. Tufte ('The Visual Display of Quantitative Information', 1983) is hostile to chartjunk and distortions of the data-ink ratio, and would prefer labelling outliers in place or a log/quantile transform over silently compressing a region. Wilkinson ('The Grammar of Graphics') formalizes scale transforms but does not endorse ad hoc breaks. Net verdict: discouraged as a default; acceptable only with very explicit visual signalling of the break. [uncertain] No authority comments on this specific plugin by name.
tradeoffs: (1) Honesty: LOW-to-MEDIUM. A compressed gap distorts perceived distance between the main cluster and outliers; without a clear break glyph readers misjudge magnitude. (2) Readability: HIGH for the dense region once configured — the whole point is to give the $0-$10K mass most of the pixel range while letting outliers still appear. (3) User comprehension: MEDIUM. A broken axis is a familiar idiom in finance/science, but the non-uniform tick spacing must be visually marked (break symbol) or naive readers misread it.
observable_urls: https://observablehq.com/@lukewhyte/handling-skewed-data-with-d3-scale-break (author's introductory notebook, purpose-built for skewed financial data; listed in fields.yaml with note 'low adoption'; Observable like count not individually published [uncertain]). Source repo: https://github.com/lukewhyte/d3-scale-break. Package: https://www.npmjs.com/package/d3-scale-break.
community_reception: Very low adoption. GitHub repo lukewhyte/d3-scale-break: 4 stars, 2 forks, last pushed 2023-01-05, not archived (verified via GitHub API on 2026-06-17). The task brief's '~10 stars' estimate overstates it; the verified figure is 4 stars / 2 forks. npm reports a few thousand weekly downloads (~4.4k cited in one source [uncertain, likely inflated by CI/mirror traffic]) at version 0.1.11. Not present in curated D3 galleries; essentially a single-author experimental utility.
code_complexity: medium. Higher than a core scale swap because you must hand-tune subdomains and scope, integrate the plugin's bespoke axis helpers, and add a visible break marker yourself. No simulation, but plugin integration plus manual breakpoint design and the maintenance risk of a pre-1.0 dependency push it above 'low'.
accessibility_support: No built-in accessibility features beyond what you add to the SVG. Apply role="img" + aria-label on the container and per-mark <title>/aria-labelledby with true dollar values. A visually-hidden data table is the recommended fallback and is especially important here: the compressed gap is a purely visual device that conveys nothing to a screen reader, so the table must expose the real, un-compressed values. [uncertain] Plugin ships no ARIA helpers.
best_for: Best when you specifically want to keep a single continuous-looking axis in dollar units yet still fit both the $0-$10K cluster (84% of 200 loans) and the 10 outliers ($120K-$1.2M) on one chart, by compressing the empty gap between ~$10K and $120K. Suits a finance audience comfortable with broken axes. Given the low adoption, unmaintained pre-1.0 status, and authority caution, prefer core alternatives (scaleSymlog for a maintained single-axis option, or scaleQuantile, or a two-panel focus+context layout) unless the explicit broken-axis idiom is a hard requirement.
